chapterSummary: Nick attends one of Gatsby's extravagant parties, where he finally meets his mysterious host and observes the superficiality and moral ambiguity of the guests. Later, he reflects on his own life in New York and reveals Jordan Baker's dishonesty, which he paradoxically finds appealing, hinting at his own evolving moral compass.

characters.name: Jay Gatsby
characters.description: The enigmatic host of lavish parties, whose true nature and past are shrouded in rumor and speculation.

knowledge.content: The superficiality and lack of genuine connection among his party guests, who often don't even know him.
He was in the First Division during the war, specifically the Sixteenth Infantry until June 1918.
The rumors circulating about him (killer, German spy, Oxford man) are largely untrue or exaggerated, yet he allows them to persist.
His wealth is sufficient to buy a hydroplane and host extravagant parties, but its origin remains mysterious.

characters.name: Nick Carraway
characters.description: Observes Gatsby's parties and finally meets his host, later reflecting on his own life and Jordan Baker's character.

knowledge.content: The uninvited nature of most of Gatsby's guests and the general atmosphere of superficiality and recklessness at the parties.
The man he has been conversing with is Jay Gatsby himself.
Gatsby was in the First Division during the war, like himself.
The rumors about Gatsby (killer, German spy, Oxford man) are contradictory and likely false, yet contribute to his mystique.
Jordan Baker is incurably dishonest, but he finds this dishonesty in women not deeply blameworthy.
His brief affair with a girl from Jersey City and his eventual decision to end it.
He considers himself one of the few honest people he knows.

characters.name: Jordan Baker
characters.description: Attends Gatsby's party with Nick, revealing more about her cynical nature and her past dishonesty.

knowledge.content: The general atmosphere of Gatsby's parties and the rumors circulating about him.
She lied about moving her ball in a golf tournament, revealing her inherent dishonesty.
She was once accused of cheating in a golf tournament, a scandal that was covered up.
Her belief that 'it takes two to make an accident,' justifying her careless driving.

characters.name: Girls in Yellow Dresses (Lucille and unnamed friend)
characters.description: Two party guests who spread rumors about Gatsby and exemplify the superficiality of the attendees.

knowledge.content: Rumors that Gatsby killed a man or was a German spy.
Gatsby's generosity in replacing a torn dress, highlighting his mysterious wealth.

characters.name: Mr. Mumble (three men)
characters.description: Three anonymous male guests at Gatsby's party, eager to hear gossip.

knowledge.content: Rumors that Gatsby was a German spy during the war.

characters.name: Owl Eyes
characters.description: A drunken, owl-eyed man found in Gatsby's library, later involved in a car accident.

knowledge.content: Gatsby's books are real, not just cardboard facades, which he finds astonishing.
He was 'brought' to the party by Mrs. Claud Roosevelt and has been drunk for a week.
He knows nothing about mechanics or driving, despite being in a car accident.

characters.name: Gatsby's Butler
characters.description: Informs Gatsby of phone calls and summons Jordan Baker.

characters.name: Orchestra Leader
characters.description: Announces Vladmir Tostoff's 'Jazz History of the World'.

characters.name: Undergraduate (Jordan's escort)
characters.description: A persistent escort of Jordan Baker, given to violent innuendo.

characters.name: Red-haired young lady from a famous chorus
characters.description: A drunken singer who weeps during her performance.

characters.name: Mrs. Sigourney Howard (Jordan's aunt)
characters.description: Jordan Baker's aunt, whose name Jordan gives to Nick for contact.

characters.name: Dangling individual
characters.description: The actual driver of the crashed car, who is oblivious to the accident.

knowledge.content: He was driving the car that crashed but is too drunk to realize it or understand the damage.

characters.name: Girl from Jersey City
characters.description: Nick's brief romantic interest from his work.

locations.name: Gatsby's Mansion
locations.description: A colossal, imitation Hôtel de Ville in Normandy, transformed into a vibrant, chaotic party venue.
lore: The site of extravagant, uninvited parties every weekend.
Features a grand library with real books, surprising many guests.
Its opulence and constant activity mask a sense of emptiness and isolation.

locations.name: Gatsby's Library
locations.description: A high Gothic library, panelled with carved English oak, containing real books.
lore: Its authenticity of books surprises guests, suggesting a deeper, perhaps unexpected, layer to Gatsby's persona.

locations.name: New York City
locations.description: The bustling metropolis where Nick works and observes the 'racy, adventurous feel' of city life.
lore: A place of anonymity and fleeting romantic encounters for Nick.
Symbolizes both opportunity and a certain haunting loneliness for its inhabitants.

locations.name: Probity Trust
locations.description: The financial institution where Nick works in the bond business.

locations.name: Yale Club
locations.description: A club where Nick dines and studies, finding it a gloomy but productive place.

locations.name: Warwick
locations.description: Location of a house-party where Jordan Baker's dishonesty is revealed.

threads.description: Nick's disillusionment with the East and its moral landscape
threads.development: Nick observes the superficiality and recklessness of Gatsby's parties, the casual dishonesty of Jordan, and the general moral decay, further deepening his disillusionment.

threads.description: The mystery surrounding Jay Gatsby and his true nature
threads.development: Nick finally meets Gatsby, but the encounter only deepens the mystery, as Gatsby's past is contradictory, and rumors about him abound. His smile and formal speech hint at a carefully constructed persona.

threads.description: The crumbling marriage and moral decay of Tom and Daisy Buchanan
threads.development: While not directly present, the East Egg guests at Gatsby's party, including Jordan's party, are described as 'rent asunder by dissension,' mirroring the moral decay seen in the Buchanan marriage.

threads.description: The social and moral contrast between East Egg and West Egg
threads.development: The chapter highlights the distinct social dynamics of West Egg (Gatsby's uninvited, chaotic parties) and East Egg (Jordan's 'stately nobility' party, condescending to West Egg), reinforcing the social divide and moral differences.

threads.description: The destructive nature of Tom Buchanan's affair with Myrtle Wilson
threads.development: This thread is dormant in this chapter, as Tom and Myrtle are not present, but the general atmosphere of moral laxity and marital discord at Gatsby's party echoes the themes of this thread.

threads.description: Nick's evolving relationship and moral judgment of Jordan Baker
threads.development: Nick spends significant time with Jordan, observing her cynicism and eventually uncovering her past dishonesty, which he finds both disturbing and strangely appealing, complicating his moral stance.

participantNames: Girls in Yellow Dresses (Lucille and unnamed friend)
Red-haired young lady from a famous chorus
scenes.summary: Nick receives a rare, formal invitation to one of Gatsby's legendary parties, a chaotic spectacle of music, champagne, and uninvited guests. He wanders uncomfortably until he finds Jordan Baker, and together they observe the superficiality and rampant gossip about their mysterious host. Nick finally meets Gatsby, who reveals himself with an 'eternal reassurance' in his smile, yet remains an enigma. As the party descends into drunken chaos, culminating in a bizarre car accident, Nick is left disturbed by the recklessness and moral ambiguity.

scenes.summary: Nick reflects on his daily life in New York, working in the bond business and experiencing a brief, casual affair that he allows to fade. He finds a certain satisfaction in the city's anonymity and energy, but also a haunting loneliness, observing similar feelings in others. This period highlights his personal struggles and observations outside of the immediate social circles of West Egg.

threadMutations.threadDescription: Nick's disillusionment with the East and its moral landscape

knowledgeMutations.content: His brief affair with a girl from Jersey City and his eventual decision to end it.

scenes.summary: Nick's relationship with Jordan Baker deepens, and he uncovers her fundamental dishonesty, recalling a past scandal where she cheated in a golf tournament. Despite this, he finds himself drawn to her, rationalizing her flaws. A conversation about her reckless driving further reveals her self-centered worldview, yet Nick's internal monologue suggests a complex, perhaps compromised, moral stance on her dishonesty.
